title: Today's AI Brief: Asana Acquires No-Code Agent-Builder Stack AI
summary: Asana acquires Stack AI, OpenAI builds self-improving tax agents with Codex
imageAlt: Image from source article for Asana acquires no-code agent-builder Stack AI
imageCaption: Asana acquires no-code agent-builder Stack AI
imageSourceTitle: Asana acquires no-code agent-builder Stack AI

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI briefing. Let's dive into the latest news. We've got a couple of interesting updates that caught my attention this week.

Asana has acquired Stack AI, a no-code agent-builder. This move helps Asana position itself as an AI-native workplace platform. Stack AI's founders, including Toni Sicilia, are joining Asana. The acquisition is part of Asana's effort to enhance its workflow automation capabilities. I think this is a smart move, as it shows Asana is serious about staying competitive in the workflow management space.

OpenAI, along with Thrive and Crete, has built a self-improving tax agent using Codex. This agent automates filings, improves accuracy, and accelerates workflows. It's an interesting example of how AI can enhance complex processes. Tax prep - not exactly the most exciting topic, but it's definitely an area where AI can make a big impact.

There's a third update that's worth mentioning - a report on automation in general. It seems like we're seeing more and more companies explore AI-powered automation. It's not hard to see why - with AI handling repetitive tasks, humans can focus on more creative and high-value work.

Asana acquires no-code agent-builder Stack AI is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Asana has acquired the workflow automation company Stack AI, the company announced on Thursday after market closed, part of a larger effort to position itself as an AI-native workplace platform. Stack AI’s founders, Tony Rosinol and Bernard Aceituno, will join Asana as part of the acquisition. The company declined to share financial terms of the deal. Asana... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Building self-improving tax agents with Codex is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: See how OpenAI, Thrive, and Crete built a self-improving tax agent with Codex, automating filings, improving accuracy, and accelerating workflows. The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
